learningGoal: Spot when both letters in a pair march forward through the alphabet together
Use the Split and Track method when both rows go the same direction

screens.title: Both letters marching forward!
screens.body:
Daisy is working through her VR practice booklet.

Did you know? Sometimes both letters in a pair go **forward** through the alphabet together — like two friends walking side by side! Split them into two rows and you'll see the pattern straight away.

**AC, BD, CE, DF, ___**

screens.title: Split and Track — both forward
screens.body: Let's split **AC, BD, CE, DF** into two rows. When both letters go forward, the pattern becomes obvious:

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Split, find patterns, then combine. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — always split into two rows first!

steps.text: First letters: A, B, C, D — goes up by 1 each time, so next is E
steps.why: Count the hops between each letter

steps.text: Second letters: C, D, E, F — goes up by 1 each time, so next is G
Combine: next pair = EG

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Use Split and Track to find the next pair:

**GI, HJ, IK, JL, ___**

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Both letters go forward by 1. First row: G, H, I, J, K. Second row: I, J, K, L, M. Next pair = KM. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is KM. Both letters go forward by 1. First row: G, H, I, J, K. Second row: I, J, K, L, M. Next pair = KM. ✓

screens.title: Both forward — nailed it!
screens.body: When both letters move forward, it's one of the friendliest patterns:

steps.text: 1. Split pairs into two rows
steps.why: First letters in one row, second letters in another

steps.text: 2. Both rows count UP
steps.why: They go the same direction — just check the step size

steps.text: 3. Apply the step to each row and combine
steps.why: Easy once you can see it! ✓

learningGoal: Understand why mixing up the two rows trips people up
Avoid the classic trap of swapping first and second letter patterns

screens.title: Is "HE" really the next pair?
screens.body:
Evie is checking her friend's VR homework. The answer given was **HE** — she mixed up which row was first and which was second.

Series (a list of letters that follow a rule): **AD, BE, CF, DG, ___**

But is that right? Press Next to find out!

screens.title: Check each row carefully!
screens.body:
The first letters are A, B, C, D (left side of each pair). The friend put the second-row answer first!

Always split first, then check each row's pattern **separately**.

steps.text: Wrong answer: HE
steps.why: she mixed up which row was first and which was second

steps.text: The first letters are A, B, C, D (left side of each pair). The friend put the second-row answer first!
Correct answer: EH
steps.why: First row: A, B, C, D → E. Second row: D, E, F, G → H. Combine = EH, not HE.

screens.title: Find the correct next pair!
screens.body:
**FI, GJ, HK, IL, ___**

Split into two rows and track each pattern:

interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! First row: F, G, H, I → J (+1). Second row: I, J, K, L → M (+1). Next pair = JM. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is JM. First row: F, G, H, I → J (+1). Second row: I, J, K, L → M (+1). Next pair = JM.

screens.title: Now you won't fall for these!
screens.body: Watch out for these common mistakes with both-forward patterns:

steps.text: Don't swap the two rows around
steps.why: First letter of each pair = first row, second letter = second row

steps.text: Don't forget to advance BOTH letters
steps.why: Both rows have their own pattern — check each one

steps.text: Don't guess the step size — count it!
steps.why: +1 and +2 look similar but give different answers ✓

learningGoal: Spot the twist: one letter goes forward while the other goes backward!
See why Split and Track is essential when letters head in opposite directions

screens.title: They're heading opposite ways!
screens.body:
Daisy is solving letter pair puzzles.

Here's the twist: one letter goes **forward** while the other goes **backward** — like two people on an escalator going in opposite directions! That's why splitting into two rows is so important.

**AZ, BY, CX, DW, ___**

screens.title: Split and Track — opposite directions
screens.body: Let's split **AZ, BY, CX, DW**. The two letters are heading in **opposite** directions — this is the most common pattern in letter pair series (a list of letter pairs that follow a rule)!

steps.text: First letters: A, B, C, D — goes forward by 1, so next is E
Second letters: Z, Y, X, W — goes backward by 1, so next is V
Combine: next pair = EV

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Split and Track — watch out for opposite directions:

**FU, GT, HS, IR, ___**

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! First letters go forward: F, G, H, I, J (+1). Second letters go backward: U, T, S, R, Q (-1). Next pair = JQ. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is JQ. First letters go forward: F, G, H, I, J (+1). Second letters go backward: U, T, S, R, Q (-1). Next pair = JQ. ✓

screens.title: Opposite directions — sorted!
screens.body: This is actually the most common pattern in the exam, so knowing it gives you a real advantage:

steps.text: 1. Split pairs into two rows
2. Check: is each row going UP or DOWN?
steps.why: They often go in opposite directions

steps.text: 3. Apply the correct direction to each row
steps.why: Forward for one, backward for the other ✓

learningGoal: Why assuming both letters go the same way leads to wrong answers
How to double-check direction for each row

screens.title: Is "EX" the right answer?
screens.body:
Evie is reviewing her friend's answer. The answer given was **EX** — she assumed both letters go forward.

Series: **AZ, BY, CX, DW, ___**

But is that right?

screens.title: Check each row's direction!
screens.body:
The first letter does go forward (A→B→C→D→E), but the second goes BACKWARD (Z→Y→X→W→V), not forward.

Always check: is each row going **up** or **down**?

steps.text: Wrong: EX
steps.why: she assumed both letters go forward

steps.text: The first letter does go forward (A→B→C→D→E), but the second goes BACKWARD (Z→Y→X→W→V), not forward.
Correct: EV
steps.why: First row: A, B, C, D → E (+1). Second row: Z, Y, X, W → V (-1). Next = EV, not EX.

screens.title: Find the correct answer!
screens.body: **GU, HT, IS, JR, ___**

interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! First row: G, H, I, J → K (+1). Second row: U, T, S, R → Q (-1). Next = KQ. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is KQ. First row: G, H, I, J → K (+1). Second row: U, T, S, R → Q (-1). Next = KQ.

screens.title: You know the trap — and you'll avoid it!
screens.body: The biggest trap in letter pair series, and how to dodge it:

steps.text: Never assume both rows go the same way
steps.why: One forward + one backward is the most common pattern

steps.text: Check direction for each row separately
steps.why: A, B, C = forward. Z, Y, X = backward

steps.text: Then apply the right step to each
steps.why: Get both letters right before combining ✓

learningGoal: Spot when letters skip one position — jumping by 2 instead of 1
Use Split and Track with +2 or -2 step patterns confidently

screens.title: These letters are skipping!
screens.body:
Grace is practising skip-one letter patterns.

Instead of moving one letter at a time, these pairs **skip one** — jumping by 2 positions each step! It's like going up stairs two at a time.

**AC, CE, EG, GI, ___**

screens.title: Split and Track — skip patterns
screens.body: Let's split **AC, CE, EG, GI**. When letters skip one, each row jumps by **2 positions** instead of 1:

interaction.sentence: When letters skip one position each time, the step size is ____
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Skipping one letter means the gap is +2 (or -2 going backward). ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — skipping one letter means jumping by 2, not 1!

steps.text: First letters: A, C, E, G — skips one each time (+2), so next is I
Second letters: C, E, G, I — skips one each time (+2), so next is K
Combine: next pair = IK

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Find the next pair — remember, these skip one!

**MO, OQ, QS, SU, ___**

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Both letters skip one (+2). First row: M, O, Q, S, U. Second row: O, Q, S, U, W. Next pair = UW. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is UW. Both letters skip one (+2). First row: M, O, Q, S, U. Second row: O, Q, S, U, W. Next pair = UW. ✓

screens.title: Skip patterns — you've got this!
screens.body: When letters skip one position, just follow the same steps:

steps.text: 1. Split pairs into two rows
2. Count the gap: A→C = skip 1 = +2
steps.why: B is skipped, so A to C is +2

steps.text: 3. Apply +2 (or -2) to find the next letter

learningGoal: Discover the constant gap — a brilliant shortcut hiding inside many series
Use the gap as a quick double-check to make sure your answer is right

screens.title: Spot the hidden gap!
screens.body:
Daisy is noticing something special about the pairs.

Here's a really neat trick: look at each pair — the **gap** between the first and second letter is **always the same**!

**AD, BE, CF, DG, ___**

Every pair has a gap of 3 between its letters. You can use this to double-check your answer!

screens.title: The constant gap shortcut
screens.body: Look at **AD, BE, CF, DG** — every pair has a gap of **3** between its letters. You can use this to double-check your answer:

steps.text: First letters: A, B, C, D — goes up by 1, so next is E
Second letters: D, E, F, G — goes up by 1, so next is H
Check: gap of 3 between E and H?
steps.why: Yes! Gap in each pair: D-A=3, E-B=3, F-C=3, G-D=3 → H-E=3 ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Find the next pair — check the gap stays the same:

**GL, HM, IN, JO, ___**

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Both letters advance by 1, keeping a constant gap of 5. G-L, H-M, I-N, J-O, K-P. Next pair = KP. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is KP. Both letters advance by 1, keeping a constant gap of 5. G-L, H-M, I-N, J-O, K-P. Next pair = KP. ✓

screens.title: The constant gap — your checking superpower!
screens.body: The constant gap is a brilliant way to double-check answers:

steps.text: 1. Count the gap between letters in each pair
steps.why: A to D = gap of 3, B to E = gap of 3

steps.text: 2. If the gap is the same every time, it's constant
steps.why: This means both rows advance at the same rate

steps.text: 3. Check your answer has the same gap
steps.why: Quick way to verify you haven't made an error ✓

learningGoal: Handle the trickiest type: when each row uses a different step size
See why Split and Track always works, even when the steps don't match

screens.title: Two different step sizes!
screens.body:
Daisy is working out a trickier letter pair series.

Here's a twist that catches a lot of people out: the first and second letters don't move by the **same amount**. One might jump by 1 while the other jumps by 2! But once you split them into rows, the pattern becomes clear.

**AF, BH, CJ, DL, ___**

screens.title: Track each row's step size
screens.body: Let's split **AF, BH, CJ, DL**. The key: each row can have its **own step size**. Don't assume they're the same!

steps.text: First letters: A, B, C, D — goes up by 1, so next is E
steps.why: Count the gap in THIS row

steps.text: Second letters: F, H, J, L — goes up by 2, so next is N
steps.why: Count the gap in THIS row — it might be different!

steps.text: Combine: next pair = EN

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Find each row's step size separately:

**AF, BH, CJ, DL, ___**

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! First letters advance by 1: A, B, C, D, E. Second letters advance by 2: F, H, J, L, N. Different step sizes! Next = EN. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is EN. First letters advance by 1: A, B, C, D, E. Second letters advance by 2: F, H, J, L, N. Different step sizes! Next = EN. ✓

screens.title: Different steps — no problem!
screens.body: When each row has a different step size, the same method still works:

steps.text: 1. Split into two rows as always
2. Count the gap in EACH row separately
steps.why: First row might be +1, second row might be +2

steps.text: 3. Don't assume they match
steps.why: +1 and +2, or +2 and -1 — anything is possible ✓

learningGoal: Recognise when the second letter is counting backward from Z
Use a quick trick to spot these patterns instantly

screens.title: Z, Y, X, W... a countdown!
screens.body:
Daisy is spotting a countdown from Z.

When you spot **Z** as the starting second letter, that's a big clue! The second row is often counting **backward** through the alphabet — like a countdown.

**AZ, BX, CV, DT, ___**

The question is: how fast is it counting back?

screens.title: Backwards from Z
screens.body: Let's split **AZ, BX, CV, DT**. When the second letter starts near Z and goes down, count the step size:

steps.text: First letters: A, B, C, D — goes forward by 1, so next is E
Second letters: Z, X, V, T — counts back by 2 from Z, so next is R
Combine: next pair = ER

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Watch for the backward count:

**AZ, BX, CV, DT, ___**

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! First letters go +1: A, B, C, D, E. Second letters count back by 2 from Z: Z, X, V, T, R. Next = ER. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is ER. First letters go +1: A, B, C, D, E. Second letters count back by 2 from Z: Z, X, V, T, R. Next = ER. ✓

screens.title: Countdown from Z — easy to spot!
screens.body: When the second letter counts back from Z, just follow these steps:

steps.text: 1. Spot the clue: second letters start at/near Z
steps.why: Z, Y, X... or Z, X, V... or Z, W, T...

steps.text: 2. Count the step size backward
3. Apply that step to find the next second letter
steps.why: Then combine with the first letter ✓

learningGoal: Handle bigger jumps of 3 or more positions without breaking a sweat
Use the numbers trick to make big jumps easy to count

screens.title: Taking bigger leaps!
screens.body:
Daisy is tackling a bigger-jump series.

Some series have letters that jump by **3 or more** positions at a time. That can feel tricky — but here's a great trick: convert letters to **numbers** (A=1, B=2, ...) and suddenly it's just adding and subtracting!

**AD, DG, GJ, JM, ___**

screens.title: Convert to numbers for big jumps
screens.body: Let's work through **AD, DG, GJ, JM**. When the jump is +3 or more, it helps to write each letter as its **alphabet number**:

bodyParts.content: Let's work through **AD, DG, GJ, JM**. When the jump is big, convert letters to numbers using the alphabet line:

steps.text: First letters: A, D, G, J — jumps by 3, so next is M
Second letters: D, G, J, M — jumps by 3, so next is P
Combine: next pair = MP
steps.why: Convert the number back to a letter ✓

interaction.sentence: When letters jump by 3 or more, convert them to ____ to make counting easier
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Converting letters to numbers (A=1, B=2... Z=26) makes big jumps much easier to count. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — convert letters to numbers (A=1, B=2...) for big jumps!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Try converting to numbers:

**AD, DG, GJ, JM, ___**

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Both letters jump by 3. First: A(1), D(4), G(7), J(10), M(13). Second: D(4), G(7), J(10), M(13), P(16). Next = MP. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is MP. Both letters jump by 3. First: A(1), D(4), G(7), J(10), M(13). Second: D(4), G(7), J(10), M(13), P(16). Next = MP. ✓

screens.title: Big jumps — no worries!
screens.body: When letters jump by 3 or more, just use numbers:

steps.text: 1. Convert letters to numbers (A=1, Z=26)
steps.why: Makes big gaps easier to count

steps.text: 2. Find the step size in each row
3. Add the step, convert back to a letter

learningGoal: Discover the magic of mirror pairs — where letter positions always add to 27!
Learn why A+Z=27, B+Y=27, C+X=27, and use it as a checking shortcut

screens.title: The magic number 27!
screens.body:
Daisy is discovering the mirror pair trick.

Did you know? There's a magic number hiding in the alphabet! In some series, the **positions of the two letters always add to 27**:

A=1, Z=26 → 1+26 = **27**
B=2, Y=25 → 2+25 = **27**

Once you know this trick, you can check your answers instantly!

**AZ, BY, CX, DW, ___**

screens.title: Mirror pairs — add to 27
screens.body: Look at **AZ, BY, CX, DW**. The alphabet has 26 letters. A mirror pair uses letters from **opposite ends**: A↔Z, B↔Y, C↔X...

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Find both letters, then verify with the 27 check. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — find each letter first, then check they add to 27!

steps.text: First letters: A(1), B(2), C(3), D(4) — next is E(5)
Second letters: Z(26), Y(25), X(24), W(23) — next is V(22)

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Use the add-to-27 shortcut:

**AZ, BY, CX, DW, ___**

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! These are mirror pairs — positions always add to 27. A(1)+Z(26)=27, B(2)+Y(25)=27, ... E(5)+V(22)=27. Next = EV. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is EV. These are mirror pairs — positions always add to 27. A(1)+Z(26)=27, B(2)+Y(25)=27, ... E(5)+V(22)=27. Next = EV. ✓

screens.title: Mirror pairs — a brilliant shortcut!
screens.body: The add-to-27 trick is one of the neatest shortcuts in VR:

steps.why: Letters from opposite ends of the alphabet

steps.text: To find the mirror: 27 minus the position
Use this to CHECK your Split and Track answer
steps.why: If the pair doesn't add to 27, something's wrong ✓

learningGoal: Spot mirror pairs quickly even when they skip — the 27 rule still works!
Use the mirror check as a speed tool in timed tests

screens.title: Do these SKIP-mirror too?
screens.body:
Evie is discovering that mirror pairs can skip too.

We know A+Z=27, B+Y=27... but what if the series **skips by 2**? Does the 27 rule still work?

**AZ, CX, EV, GT, ___**

Let's find out!

screens.title: The 27 rule works with skips!
screens.body: Let's check **AZ, CX, EV, GT**. Even when both rows skip by 2, each pair still adds to 27:

bodyParts.content: Let's check **AZ, CX, EV, GT**. Even with skips, each pair still adds to 27!

steps.text: First letters: A(1), C(3), E(5), G(7) — skips by 2, next is I(9)
Second letters: Z(26), X(24), V(22), T(20) — skips back by 2, next is R(18)
Check: Still mirror pairs! Each adds to 27, but they skip by 2

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Use the 27 shortcut to check your answer:

**AZ, CX, EV, GT, ___**

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Skip-mirror! First +2: A, C, E, G, I. Second -2: Z, X, V, T, R. Every pair still adds to 27. Next = IR. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is IR. Skip-mirror! First +2: A, C, E, G, I. Second -2: Z, X, V, T, R. Every pair still adds to 27. Next = IR. ✓

screens.title: The 27 trick works every time!
screens.body: The 27 shortcut works with ANY step size — how brilliant is that?

steps.text: +1/-1 mirrors: AZ, BY, CX, DW...
+2/-2 mirrors: AZ, CX, EV, GT...
Quick check: first letter position + second = 27?
steps.why: If yes, you've got the right answer ✓
